chapters.title: A holiday that belongs to everyone
chapters.text:
When Melanie Kirkpatrick visited Newcomers High School in Queens, a public school that serves mostly children from immigrant families, she planned to share the history of Thanksgiving and listen to how the students marked the holiday at home.

She quickly saw that the holiday's values touched the students deeply. They began treating it as an anchor in their new lives in America. Different as their backgrounds were, the holiday carried the same meaning for all of them, a sense of belonging, community support, and hope for the future. They folded traditions from their home countries into American customs. A Polish family, for example, served pierogi next to their turkey.

In a globalized world, weaving the traditions of several cultures into one celebration adds real meaning.

Turkey, parades, family gatherings, and giving thanks are the familiar beats of the day. Behind each one there are personal stories waiting to be told, both in moments of joy and in moments of loss. Walk with me and their stories will come along.

chapters.title: How Thanksgiving came to be Thanksgiving
chapters.text:
How well do you know the Thanksgiving story. The famous 1621 celebration in Plymouth, where two peoples came together, was not actually America's first Thanksgiving. The fuller story turns out to be more interesting.

The Pilgrims, who were mostly Calvinists, endured a long and brutal journey to the shores of New England and lost many members of their community along the way. Every year brought a fight to set aside enough food for winter, so each day was a chance to give thanks. They prayed and thanked God throughout the day, every morning, between work, before meals, and before bed. What we now call Thanksgiving would have been just another day of thanks for them.

That 1621 gathering, and the ones that came after, gave the Pilgrims a chance to strengthen ties with local tribes, pick up new knowledge, and try to convert Native Americans to Christianity.

It is also worth remembering that the timing and shape of these gatherings varied across what would become the United States. The Massachusetts Bay Colony, for example, held its own version of Thanksgiving as early as 1630.

Even the Dutch, who explored the continent and built New Netherland in what is now New York City, started similar gatherings in the 1640s. They used these peaceful events to rebuild ties after conflicts with Native Americans.

Before it became the unifying holiday we know today, Thanksgiving went through many dark chapters and double standards. The widely loved painting The First Thanksgiving at Plymouth by Jennie Brownscombe, painted in 1914, contains several inaccuracies. The landscape suggests a late autumn setting, although the first gathering happened in late summer, right after the harvest. The painting also shows women eating with Native Americans and Pilgrims, but they were more likely busy preparing the meal. The Native Americans in the painting wear bright feathered headdresses typical of western tribes rather than the traditional dress of New England's Indigenous peoples.

chapters.title: When the president set the example
chapters.text:
Skip ahead a few centuries to another pivotal moment. In 1789, George Washington, the founder and first president of the United States, declared a national Thanksgiving celebration. As if he sensed he was about to set an example for the future, he approached the task with wisdom and a sense of inclusion.

Shared traditions help a community form one vision when they are building something new, even a country.

His proclamation called for unity and thanked God for supporting the new nation, asking for blessings on the daily work of its people. Notably, he did not single out any religion or nationality. His words embraced everyone in the country. He also made a generous gesture by donating to a church and encouraging others to do good.

Washington's speech launched what became an annual tradition of Thanksgiving proclamations by US presidents. Only Thomas Jefferson pushed back, arguing that the Constitution barred presidents from promoting religious messages or practices, even when those messages were meant to preserve tradition rather than promote the president's faith.

Jefferson also pointed out that there was no penalty for not celebrating Thanksgiving. He worried that formal presidential proclamations could feed public skepticism and weaken the office. Why Jefferson focused so tightly on the details of the proclamations instead of encouraging unity remains a mystery. What is clear today is that his arguments did not persuade every future president. Following Washington's unofficial template, Abraham Lincoln mentioned God in his Thanksgiving speech, Theodore Roosevelt spoke of the greatest Giver, and Franklin Roosevelt thanked the father of all people.

That line about self-sufficiency and gratitude comes from an old Educational Research Bulletin piece on Thanksgiving Day.

chapters.title: Thank you, Sarah
chapters.text:
Thanksgiving becoming a national holiday was shaped not only by heads of state but also by some unexpected figures who left their mark in quieter ways.

One such person was Sarah Josepha Hale, a name rarely found on lists of famous feminists, powerful editors, or Thanksgiving history. Born in 1788 to parents who believed in educating sons and daughters equally, she picked up a college level education through her brother's guidance.

Ignoring what society expected, she waited until she was twenty five to marry, considered late at the time. She married a lawyer, and the couple spent two hours each evening studying literature, French, and biology together.

After ten years of marriage, her husband died and left her alone with four children. That challenge pushed her writing forward. After publishing a powerful novel about slavery, Northwood: A Tale of New England, the text caught the eye of a magazine publisher, Godey's Lady's Book, leading to a forty year role as its editor. During her tenure, the magazine became the most famous periodical in the country before the Civil War.

Godey's Lady's Book became her platform for promoting Thanksgiving, which she cherished, as a national holiday. For Hale, the unifying power of the holiday could help reduce racism and sexism, lift patriotism, and bridge America's political divides.

Sincere enthusiasm for someone's work inspires others to find creative paths to their goals.

To spread the idea, she used articles featuring holiday recipes and short stories about cozy family weekends that many readers enjoyed. As readers pictured themselves in those warm scenes, sharing laughs around a table with a juicy turkey, the holiday's appeal grew.

Even during the Civil War, the magazine kept its focus on Thanksgiving. The number of states embracing the celebration kept rising, undeterred by the national conflict.

chapters.title: The Pilgrims' teacher
chapters.text:
What do Native Americans think about Thanksgiving. Their ancestors were the ones who first taught the Pilgrims how to live in the new conditions, only to later be forced to fight for their lands and face genocide.

Before answering that question, step back in time with me. Some Native Americans engaged with European explorers. One of them was Squanto, also called Tisquantum, of the Patuxet tribe of the Wampanoags. His first contact with Europeans began with kidnapping, a common practice at the time. Sailors back then looked for new trade routes and lands, but they also captured locals to sell into slavery or display as curiosities in European exhibitions.

Squanto spent nearly a decade in England. Historians do not know exactly what he was forced to do there, but he ended up learning English and returned to his tribe with an expedition. Not long after, he was kidnapped again. This time he would have been enslaved in Spain if Franciscan friars had not rescued him, converted him to Christianity, and helped him find his way home. No one was waiting for him. His entire community had died from an unknown disease after contact with Europeans.

The very next year, he met the Pilgrims who had settled near his village. Knowing the language and carrying valuable knowledge, he became their teacher and mentor, paying back the cruelty he had faced with kindness.

During some celebrations, it matters to enjoy being together and remember those who made it possible.

For generations, Native American perspectives on Thanksgiving went unheard. Today, their descendants generally take one of three approaches to the holiday. Some hold a classic Thanksgiving celebration with turkey and a parade. Some spend a family evening exploring the cultures and traditions of various Indigenous communities. Some treat the day as one of remembrance and mourning for the victims of genocide. Each choice deserves respect given what their ancestors went through.

chapters.title: Turning attention outward
chapters.text:
Over time, Thanksgiving grew to honor both togetherness and a sense of duty to those facing hardship.

Remember the Friends episode where Phoebe used every trick she could think of to gather donations for children. Her over the top volunteer spirit and her skill at wishing Merry Christmas in different languages captured something real about American giving culture.

The joy of helping others and the gratitude people feel in return can be worth more than material rewards.

The first documented mention of Thanksgiving related charity dates back to 1636, when a Massachusetts church hosted a dinner and invited the public. Over time, similar events became standard for many religious organizations around the country.

Since the 19th century, charitable organizations like The Bowery Mission and The Salvation Army have focused on supporting people in need. These groups helped build and popularize the volunteer movement, pulling together people willing to donate both money and time to help others.

There is another side to this holiday. While Thanksgiving celebrates gratitude and giving, many see Black Friday as its opposite. The day's focus on extreme consumerism and greed pushes people to chase deals rather than share with others.

In response to Black Friday, activists created the Giving Tuesday initiative, which has spread around the world and now operates in 154 countries. The movement encourages businesses and organizations to launch meaningful social projects and share their results on social networks. Since its creation in 2012, major companies like Microsoft, PayPal, Tinder, and Bitcoin have joined in.

All these efforts would surely warm Sarah Josepha Hale's heart. Although Thanksgiving did not become the global holiday she dreamed of, its spirit of giving lives on in projects around the world.

That line about looking back on the year past is from a letter by Abigail Adams.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
History is made up of many distinct names and actions. Building a brighter future means remembering our past, asking tough questions, and accepting the truth, no matter how bitter it may be. The same spirit applies to the holidays we choose and the way we celebrate them.

Try this. Do not save gratitude only for Thanksgiving. Looking for reasons to be thankful on a regular basis boosts happiness and motivation. Start small and ask yourself what you are grateful for today. Create a new family tradition and pick a place to volunteer. A few hours of your time can make a difference in someone else's life. Keep exploring the holiday's origins, especially through Native American perspectives. Watch documentaries, visit cultural museums, or research the myths and folklore of different tribes. Plan your Black Friday shopping months ahead to avoid impulse purchases you may regret.
